characterCreation.title: Name Your Captain
characterCreation.nameRequired: Please enter a name to continue

step1.title: You ARE the Pet
step1.desc: Use arrow keys to move around the grid. Find the treat!
step1.foundSub: That was easy when you can see everything, right?

step2.title: Now You're Blindfolded
step2.desc: The screen goes dark. Can you find the treat from memory?
step2.blindfolded: You can only see nearby cells!
step2.found: You found it in the dark!
step2.foundSub: That was harder, right? What if your pet could LEARN which direction is usually good?

step3.title: The Learning Loop
step3.desc: Watch your pet learn to find the treat on its own!
step3.memoryDesc: Brighter = pet thinks this spot leads to treats

step4.title: The Big Question
step4.congrats: You just saw the core of ALL reinforcement learning!
step4.desc: There are MANY ways to teach your pet to learn. Each island teaches a different method. Which route will you sail first?
step4.concept_agent_desc: Your pet — the learner who makes decisions
step4.concept_state_desc: Where you are — the current situation
step4.concept_action_desc: What you do — the choice you make
step4.concept_reward_desc: Feedback — did it work well or badly?

prologue.line1: In the Sea of Intelligence, a great mystery awaits: the Optimal Strategy.
prologue.line2: Many captains sailed in search of it. None returned.
prologue.line3: A dark force — the Chaos Engine — corrupts these waters.
prologue.line4: One new captain, and their learning companion, must set it right.

villain.teaser: ⚠️ The Chaos Engine corrupts these waters...

map.title: The Sea of Intelligence
map.hardRouteWarning: This route explores advanced ideas. We recommend starting with Value Archipelago — but you're the captain! Ready to set sail?

bandit.name: Port Bandit
bandit.metaphor: A row of treasure chests — which one pays out the most?

qtable.name: Port Q-Table
qtable.metaphor: Drawing a treasure map — mark how good each spot is.

deep.name: Port Deep
deep.metaphor: The treasure map is too big to draw by hand — train a pattern recognition assistant!

boss.name: The Greedy Pirate
boss.desc: A pirate who ALWAYS picks the highest immediate reward. Can your trained agent outperform pure greed?

sarsa.name: Port SARSA
sarsa.metaphor: The cautious explorer — learns from what it actually does, not what it could do

doubledqn.name: Port Twin
doubledqn.metaphor: Don't trust a single opinion — two evaluators to avoid overconfidence

duelingdqn.name: Port Duel
duelingdqn.metaphor: Split 'how good is this place?' from 'how good is this action here?'

bandit.story: You arrive at a mysterious cove lined with treasure chests. Each chest glows with a different color. A weathered pirate leans against a palm tree and grins: 'Five chests, one hundred chances. Think you can figure out which one pays the most?'
bandit.primer: What is probability? Think of it like a weather forecast. When they say '70% chance of rain,' they mean if today happened 100 times, it would rain about 70 of those times. Each treasure chest has its own hidden probability of giving you gold.
bandit.summary: You learned the Multi-Armed Bandit problem — the art of balancing exploration (trying new things) with exploitation (sticking with what works). The epsilon-greedy strategy helps: most of the time pick the best option, but occasionally try something new.

dialogue.1: Captain! We've arrived at Treasure Cove. See those glowing chests? The Chaos Engine cursed them — it wants us to open the same one every time!
dialogue.2: Five chests, a hundred chances. Think you can figure out which one pays the most without wasting all your turns?
dialogue.3: This is the classic explore vs. exploit problem. Open too few different chests, you'll miss the good one. Open too many, you'll waste your turns. We need a smart strategy!

bandit.bridge: Good work, Captain! But what if each spot could lead somewhere else, with different treasures? We need an algorithm that handles sequences of decisions — say hello to Q-Learning!

qtable.story: You reach a cursed island covered in fog. An old sailor warns you: 'This island shifts and traps those who wander blindly. Only a true cartographer — one who maps every path — can escape.'
qtable.primer: What is a table of values? Think of a spreadsheet where each row is a location and each column is a direction. The numbers tell you how good each choice is.
qtable.summary: You learned Q-Learning — an algorithm that builds a complete map of how good every action is at every location. By balancing exploration and exploitation, it finds the optimal path through any grid world. The key insight: ask your future self for advice (the Bellman equation).
qtable.bridge: Q-Learning is great but plays it a bit too bold near danger. What if the algorithm learned only from what it *actually* does — risky moves included? That's SARSA's secret.

deep.story: You've mapped the treasure-filled islands, but now you face a vast ocean of possibilities. An old navigator says: 'When the map becomes too big to draw, you need a brain that can see patterns.' Welcome to the world of neural networks.
deep.primer: What is a neural network? Think of it like a smart pattern-matching machine. Just like your brain recognizes faces without checking every pixel, a neural network learns to recognize patterns in data. It has layers of 'neurons' that pass information forward, getting smarter with each example it sees.
deep.summary: You learned DQN — Deep Q-Network — which replaces the Q-table with a neural network that can handle huge state spaces. Two key tricks make it work: Experience Replay (studying from shuffled memories) and a Target Network (having a stable teacher). DQN was the first algorithm to master Atari games!
deep.bridge: DQN works, but it tends to overestimate how good actions are. What if two networks kept each other honest? Double DQN brings a second opinion.

sarsa.story: You reach a mountain trail with a shortcut along the cliff edge. A veteran explorer warns you: 'The fast path is tempting, but it's dangerous. A wise navigator learns from every slip — not just the theoretical best move.' Enter SARSA, the cautious algorithm.
sarsa.primer: What is on-policy learning? It means your algorithm learns from the actions it actually takes — including risky exploratory moves. This makes it more realistic about the dangers ahead.
sarsa.summary: SARSA learns from what it actually does, not what it could theoretically do. This on-policy approach makes it naturally more cautious near dangerous areas. The tiny change from Q-Learning (using the actual next action instead of the greedy best) leads to surprisingly different behavior!
sarsa.bridge: SARSA is cautious, but its Q-table has millions of rows in a big world — it would explode! What if we used a neural network to summarize all that knowledge? Enter DQN.

doubledqn.story: You find an ancient oracle who claims to know the value of every treasure. But whispers say the oracle always gives the highest estimate — never an honest one. To find the truth, you need a second opinion. Enter Double DQN: the algorithm that checks its own optimism.
doubledqn.primer: What is overestimation bias? When you always take the maximum of noisy estimates, you tend to overestimate. It's like asking a salesperson how good their product is — they'll always say great!
doubledqn.summary: Double DQN fixes DQN's overestimation problem by separating which action to pick from how to evaluate it. The online network selects the action; the target network evaluates it. This tiny change reduces bias and often leads to better-performing agents.
doubledqn.bridge: We've fixed overconfidence. But here's a deeper question: is a situation good because of WHERE you are, or WHAT you do there? Dueling DQN separates the two.

duelingdqn.story: You've learned to score actions. But there's a deeper question: is an action good because of WHERE you are, or WHAT you did? A wise analyst splits the two — understanding a state's inherent value separately from the advantage of each action. Enter Dueling DQN.
duelingdqn.primer: What is state value? It's how good it is to just BE in a state, regardless of what you do next. Some states are inherently bad (near a cliff), others are inherently good (near the exit). This separation helps the network learn more efficiently.
duelingdqn.summary: Dueling DQN splits Q-values into state value V(s) and advantage A(s,a). This lets the network efficiently learn which states are good (even when actions barely differ) and which actions stand out. The result: better Q-value estimates with the same training data.
duelingdqn.bridge: You've mastered all the value methods! But there's another approach entirely — instead of scoring positions, what if the algorithm directly learned to throw better darts? That's the idea behind Policy Gradient methods on Volcanic Isle.

reinforce.story: You've reached the foot of the Volcanic Isle. A lone archer stands on the cliff, throwing darts at a target. She misses. She misses again. Then — thwack — a bullseye! She grins: 'Every hit, I throw more like that. Every miss, I throw less. That's all I do.' You've just witnessed REINFORCE.
reinforce.primer: What is a probability distribution? Think of it like a spinner on a board game. Instead of landing on one fixed spot, the policy has different-sized slices for each action. The bigger the slice, the more often you pick that action. Learning means reshaping the slices.
reinforce.summary: You learned REINFORCE — the simplest policy gradient method. Instead of scoring positions, it directly tweaks the probability of each action. Good episodes make their actions more likely; bad episodes make them less likely. It's noisy but elegant — the foundation of all policy gradient methods.

dialogue.1: Welcome to Policy Volcanic Isle! The Chaos Engine made this place unstable — it wants every policy to be random and chaotic. We need algorithms that can learn under pressure!
dialogue.2: I've watched this volcano for decades. The key insight: don't score every spot. Just keep track of what moves lead to success, and do more of those.
dialogue.3: This is REINFORCE — pure policy gradient. Direct, simple, elegant. But watch out for the noise. The volcano tests your patience.

reinforce.bridge: REINFORCE is elegant but very noisy — like learning from a single coin flip each time. What if we had a critic to reduce that noise? Actor-Critic splits the learning into two roles.

actorcritic.story: Deep in the Volcanic Isle, a martial arts master trains her apprentice. 'Strike left!' the apprentice acts. 'Good — but your stance was weak,' the master critiques. With every exchange, the apprentice's moves sharpen and the master's eye sharpens too. They learn together.
actorcritic.primer: What is a baseline? When you say 'that was better than average,' you need to know what average is first. A baseline is that average — it tells us whether a result was surprisingly good or surprisingly bad. Subtracting the baseline from rewards reduces noise in learning.
actorcritic.summary: Actor-Critic combines the best of both worlds: the Actor directly optimizes the policy, while the Critic estimates state values to reduce variance. Instead of waiting for a full episode (like REINFORCE), the TD error signal lets learning happen at every step — faster and more stable.
actorcritic.bridge: One actor-critic pair is good. But what if a hundred workers explored in parallel and pooled their experience? That's A2C — Advantage Actor-Critic with parallel workers.

a2c.story: The volcanic summit comes into view. An army of robot soldiers fans out across the slopes, each exploring a different path. They share everything they learn. An individual might wander for hours; together, they conquer in minutes. This is the power of parallel learning.
a2c.primer: What is the advantage function? Instead of asking 'how good was this action?', advantage asks 'how much BETTER was this action than what I usually do here?' A(s,a) = Q(s,a) - V(s). Positive advantage: better than expected. Negative: worse.
a2c.summary: A2C (Advantage Actor-Critic) extends Actor-Critic with parallel workers that share a single policy. Each worker explores independently, then contributes gradients to the shared update. The Advantage function reduces variance further, and synchronous updates keep learning stable.
a2c.bridge: A2C is powerful, but big updates can sometimes destabilize training. PPO adds guardrails — it clips updates to stay close to the last policy, making training much more robust.

ppo.story: You reach the volcanic throne. The final guardian is a patient strategist who has tried every trick — and learned from every mistake. 'I update slowly,' she says, 'but every step forward stays forward. No more crashing back to square one.' This is PPO.
ppo.primer: What is a clipping function? Imagine you want to change a setting, but only allow changes within a 20% range. If someone says 'change by 50%', you clip it to 20%. PPO uses this to prevent any single update from being too aggressive.
ppo.summary: PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) is one of the most widely used RL algorithms today. It adds a clipping mechanism to prevent updates from being too large, so training stays stable. The clip range ε controls the learning speed — too wide causes chaos, too narrow causes stagnation.
ppo.bridge: You've mastered Policy Volcanic Isle! PPO is still one of the most widely-used RL algorithms today. The Chaos Volcano awaits your final test!

story.dialogueDone: Ready to learn how this works?

feel.title: Feel the Problem
feel.instruction: Click a treasure chest to open it! You have 20 turns to earn as much gold as possible.
feel.bestChest: The best chest was #{{chest}}. Did you figure it out?
feel.segue: Now let's see how an algorithm tackles this...

meet.title: Meet the Algorithm
meet.slide0: You have 5 treasure chests. How would you decide which one to open next?
meet.slide1a: Simple idea: always open the chest that has given the most gold so far.
meet.slide1b: But what if you got unlucky on your first try? You might miss the real best chest forever!
meet.slide2desc: Step {{steps}}/15 — The greedy strategy only picks what looks best...
meet.slide2note: Notice how it gets stuck on one chest? It never checks if other chests might be better.
meet.slide3a: What if you spent MOST of your turns on the best chest, but SOMETIMES tried a random one?
meet.slide3b: Think of it like this: You usually eat at your favorite restaurant, but once in a while you try a new place. That's how you discovered your favorite in the first place!
meet.slide3c: This balance between 'sticking with what works' and 'trying something new' is called the Exploration vs Exploitation tradeoff.
meet.slide4desc: Epsilon controls how often the algorithm explores. Drag the slider to see the balance change:
meet.epsilonExplain: {{exploit}}% of the time: pick the best known chest. {{explore}}% of the time: try a random chest.
meet.noExplore: Zero exploration — you might miss the best chest forever!
meet.allExplore: All random — you never use what you've learned!
meet.slide5desc: This strategy is called epsilon-greedy. With a small epsilon like 10%, you mostly exploit your knowledge but keep discovering.
meet.slide5formula: With probability epsilon: explore (random) | Otherwise: exploit (pick best)
meet.slide5ready: Ready to watch the algorithm learn on its own? Let's go!

watch.title: Watch It Learn
watch.desc: The algorithm picks chests on its own. Watch the values update in real-time!

quest.briefing: Your quest: Earn as much gold as possible in 100 turns! Choose your strategy wisely — the chests hide a secret...
quest.rule1: 5 treasure chests, each with a hidden payout rate
quest.rule2: 100 turns to maximize your total gold
quest.rule3: TWIST: Chest payouts CHANGE at turn 50!
quest.configDesc: Pick a strategy and tune its settings. Then send your algorithm on the quest!
quest.tipEpsilon: Tip: A moderate epsilon (10-20%) balances exploration and exploitation. Too low and you'll miss changes at turn 50!
quest.tipUCB: Tip: UCB automatically explores uncertain chests. A moderate c (1-3) usually works well.
quest.shiftDesc: Chest payouts have shifted! Can your algorithm adapt?
quest.passed: Quest Complete! Your algorithm outsmarted random chance.
quest.failed: Not quite there. Try different settings and retry — it's free!

feel.title: Feel the Problem
feel.instruction: Navigate the grid to find the exit! Use arrow keys, WASD, or click adjacent cells.
feel.controls: Arrow keys / WASD to move, or click an adjacent cell
feel.didntEscape: You couldn't find the exit in time. Navigating a maze is harder than it looks!
feel.segue: What if an algorithm could learn the best path by trying thousands of times? Let's meet Q-Learning...

meet.title: Meet Q-Learning
meet.slide0: What if you could draw a map of how good each direction is at every spot? That's exactly what Q-Learning does!
meet.slide1a: Imagine a big spreadsheet. Each row is a position on the grid. Each column is a direction (up, right, down, left). The numbers inside are scores — how good is it to go that direction from that spot?
meet.slide1b: At the start, all scores are 0 — the agent knows nothing. As it explores, the scores fill in based on experience.
meet.slide2title: Ask Your Future Self for Advice
meet.slide2a: Here's the clever part: when you get a reward, don't just update THIS spot. Also peek at the next spot and ask: 'What's the best thing I can do from there?'
meet.slide2b: It's like asking a friend who already explored ahead: 'Hey, if I go right, what's the best score I can get from there?'
meet.slide2c: This is called the Bellman equation — a fancy name for a simple idea:
meet.slide3title: Watch It Learn Step by Step
meet.slide3desc: Step {{step}} | Episode {{episode}} — Watch the Q-values (colors) update in real-time on this tiny 3x3 grid!
meet.slide3note: Warm colors = high value, cool colors = low value. Arrows show the best action per cell.
meet.slide4desc: The learning rate controls how much each experience changes the Q-table. Too fast = chaotic. Too slow = takes forever.
meet.lrLow: Very cautious! Each experience barely changes the map. Stable, but slow to learn.
meet.lrHigh: Very aggressive! The map swings wildly with each experience. Fast but chaotic.
meet.lrMedium: A nice balance. The map updates steadily without overreacting.
meet.slide5title: Q-Learning: Build a Complete Treasure Map!
meet.slide5desc: Q-Learning builds a complete map of the best moves. Given enough exploration, it finds the optimal path through any grid world.
meet.slide5ready: Ready to watch Q-Learning tackle a real maze? Let's go!

watch.title: Watch It Learn
watch.desc: Q-Learning explores the grid and builds its treasure map. Watch the Q-values update in real-time!
watch.qtableDesc: Each cell is split into 4 triangles (up/right/down/left). Brighter = higher Q-value.
watch.rewardDesc: Total reward per episode. Higher is better — watch it improve over time!

quest.briefing: A cursed island with traps, treasure, and a hidden exit. First YOU try to escape, then Q-Learning gives it a shot. Can the algorithm beat your path?
quest.rule1: 6x8 grid with walls, traps (-5), treasures (+5), and an exit (+10)
quest.rule2: You get 20 moves to try escaping manually
quest.rule3: Then Q-Learning trains and tries to find a shorter path
quest.manualDesc: Navigate to the exit (green F). Use arrow keys or click adjacent cells.
quest.manualFailed: Out of moves! You didn't reach the exit.
quest.manualBenchmark: Your benchmark: {{length}} steps. Can Q-Learning do better?
quest.configDesc: Your benchmark is {{benchmark}} steps. Tune the hyperparameters and see if Q-Learning can find a shorter path!
quest.tip: Tip: A learning rate around 0.1-0.3 with discount 0.9-0.99 usually works well. More episodes = better results.
quest.passed: Q-Learning found a shorter path than yours! The algorithm learned the optimal route.
quest.failed: Q-Learning couldn't beat your path. Try more episodes or different settings!

feel.title: Feel the Problem
feel.intro: Try to break all the bricks! Use the paddle to bounce the ball. Can you clear the board?
feel.controls: Use arrow keys or move your mouse to control the paddle.
feel.controlHint: Arrow keys or mouse to move paddle
feel.resultMessage: Now let's see if a neural network can learn to beat you!

slide1.title: The Map Is Too Big!
slide1.content: Remember Q-Learning's treasure map? For a pixel game, the map would need MILLIONS of entries — one for every possible screen. We can't draw that by hand!

slide2.title: Meet the Pattern Matcher
slide2.content: Instead of a giant table, we use a neural network — a pattern-matching machine. Give it the game screen, and it tells you how good each action is. It's like having a smart assistant who can read any map!

slide3.title: Studying from a Shuffled Notebook
slide3.content: You know how shuffling flashcards helps you study better? The AI keeps a notebook of memories and studies them in random order. This is called Experience Replay — it prevents the AI from only remembering the last thing that happened.

slide4.title: Having a Stable Teacher
slide4.content: Imagine if your teacher's answers changed every second — confusing, right? DQN keeps a stable 'teacher' network that only updates occasionally. The 'student' learns fast, then the teacher slowly catches up.

slide5.title: How It All Works Together
slide5.content: DQN combines four ideas into one powerful learner:
slide5.step1: Play the game and remember what happened
slide5.step2: Shuffle memories and study a random batch
slide5.step3: Use the neural network to predict Q-values
slide5.step4: Periodically copy to the stable teacher

slide6.title: DQN = Q-Learning + Brain Power
slide6.content: Deep Q-Network takes Q-Learning's idea of 'scoring every action' and supercharges it with a neural network, shuffled studying, and a stable teacher. Let's see it in action!

quest.title: Pixel Arcade Challenge
quest.briefing: Beat your own high score with AI! First, play Breakout yourself. Then, configure and train a DQN agent to beat you.
quest.step1: 1. Play Breakout manually — set your benchmark
quest.step2: 2. Configure the DQN — tune learning rate & memory
quest.step3: 3. Train and watch — can the AI beat your score?
quest.manualPhase: Your Turn — Set a High Score!
quest.configIntro: Now configure the DQN agent. Choose your hyperparameters wisely!
quest.resultMessage: DQN combines a neural network with shuffled studying and a stable teacher to master games that are too complex for a simple table!

pirate.title: BOSS: The Greedy Pirate
pirate.name: The Greedy Pirate
pirate.dialogue1: I always grab the closest gold! No one can out-earn me!
pirate.dialogue2: You think your fancy 'learning' can beat my instincts? Bring it on!
pirate.challengeDesc: Train a Q-Learning agent to earn more total gold than the Greedy Pirate on a maze with hidden treasure.
pirate.pirateRunDesc: Watch: the pirate grabs every nearby coin without thinking ahead.
pirate.pirateExplain: The pirate grabbed every nearby coin, but missed the big treasure. Can you do better?
pirate.victoryMessage: Your agent learned that patience beats greed! Long-term thinking led to the big treasure.
pirate.defeatMessage: The pirate wins this time. Try training longer, or increase the Discount Factor so your agent values future rewards more!
pirate.lessonLearned: The secret: an agent that values the future can outperform one that only grabs what's right in front of it.

cards.title: Algorithm Card Gallery
cards.selectHint: Select a card to see details

feel.title: The Cliff Walk
feel.instruction: Navigate from start to exit — but beware the cliff edge! You have 30 moves.
feel.segue: Why does taking the safe path matter? Let's see how SARSA handles it differently from Q-Learning...

meet.slide0title: Wait, Didn't We Already Learn Q-Learning?
meet.slide0desc: Q-Learning is great, but it has a blind spot — it always imagines the best possible future action, even if it wouldn't actually take that action. SARSA asks: what if we learned from what we actually do?
meet.slide0example: Imagine Q-Learning is confident near a cliff because it imagines a perfect step. SARSA remembers it actually slips sometimes!
meet.slide1a: Q-Learning is 'off-policy' — it learns from the best possible action (greedy), even if it explores.
meet.slide1b: SARSA is 'on-policy' — it learns from the action it actually chose, including random exploration moves.
meet.slide1analogy: It's like: Q-Learning studies from someone else's perfect diary. SARSA studies from its own actual diary.
meet.slide2a: Both use almost the same formula, but with one key difference:
meet.slide2formula1: Q-Learning: Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + α[r + γ × max Q(s',a') − Q(s,a)]
meet.slide2formula2: SARSA:      Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + α[r + γ × Q(s', a') − Q(s,a)]
meet.slide2note: In SARSA, a' is the NEXT action the policy will actually take — not just the theoretical best!
meet.slide3desc: On the cliff walk, Q-Learning learns: 'Running along the edge is optimal!' (because the best path is optimal). SARSA learns: 'Running along the edge is dangerous!' (because it slips off sometimes during exploration).
meet.slide3risky: Q-Learning may risk the cliff edge
meet.slide4a: SARSA is more cautious — it avoids risky paths that might lead to disaster during exploration.
meet.slide4b: Q-Learning is more optimistic — it finds the theoretically best path.
meet.slide4c: Which is better? Depends on the environment! For safety-critical tasks, SARSA wins.
meet.summary1: SARSA is on-policy: it learns from what it actually does, including exploration mistakes
meet.summary2: Near cliffs and danger zones, SARSA is more conservative — which can be a strength
meet.summary3: The only difference from Q-Learning: use the actual next action, not the greedy best

watch.title: SARSA vs Q-Learning
watch.desc: Watch both train on the same cliff grid. Which takes the safer path?

quest.title: Cliffside Race
quest.briefing: Train SARSA and Q-Learning on a cliff walk. Which algorithm stays safer near the dangerous edge?
quest.rule1: Both algorithms train on the same cliff-edge grid
quest.rule2: Traps represent the cliff edge — falling off costs big!
quest.rule3: SARSA should learn to avoid the cliff, Q-Learning might risk it
quest.passed: Quest Complete! You saw how on-policy learning shapes behavior.

feel.title: The Overestimation Trap
feel.instruction: Navigate the grid. Notice: some paths look better than they actually are!
feel.teaserDesc: DQN can overestimate Q-values — it thinks some actions are way better than they really are. This leads to poor decisions!
feel.segue: What if we could fix DQN's overestimation? Let's meet Double DQN...

meet.slide0title: DQN Has a Flaw: It's Too Optimistic!
meet.slide0desc: Remember how DQN uses a target network? There's a problem: when picking the best future Q-value, DQN always picks the maximum — and maximums are often overestimated.
meet.slide0example: It's like asking your most optimistic friend to rate every restaurant — they'll always say '10/10!' even for bad ones.
meet.slide1a: DQN computes the target like this:
meet.slide1formula: Target = r + γ × max_a' Q_target(s', a')
meet.slide1b: The problem: using the same network to both SELECT the best action AND EVALUATE it creates an upward bias. The max tends to be too high!
meet.slide2title: Double DQN: Two Networks, Better Estimates
meet.slide2a: The fix is elegant: use the online network to SELECT which action is best, but use the target network to EVALUATE that action's value.
meet.slide2formula1: Step 1: a* = argmax Q_online(s', a')  [online picks the action]
meet.slide2formula2: Step 2: Target = r + γ × Q_target(s', a*)  [target evaluates it]
meet.slide2b: This decoupling breaks the overestimation cycle!
meet.slide3desc: Same state, three scenarios. DQN overestimates, Double DQN is more accurate:
meet.slide4a: Overestimated Q-values lead to overconfident policies — the agent thinks it's doing better than it is.
meet.slide4b: With more accurate values, Double DQN often finds better policies, especially in harder environments.
meet.slide4c: The change is tiny (just 2 lines of code) but the impact can be significant!
meet.summary1: Double DQN decouples action selection from action evaluation
meet.summary2: The online network picks the best action; the target network evaluates it
meet.summary3: Small change, real improvement — especially in complex environments

watch.title: DQN vs Double DQN
watch.desc: Watch both train side by side. Double DQN should show less overestimation!

quest.title: The Skeptic's Trial
quest.briefing: Train DQN and Double DQN on the same environment. Double DQN should match or beat DQN's performance with less overestimation.
quest.rule1: Both algorithms train on the same grid environment
quest.rule2: Double DQN must match at least 95% of DQN's average reward
quest.rule3: Watch the overestimation gap — Double DQN stays more accurate
quest.passed: The Skeptic approves! Double DQN matched DQN's performance with better accuracy.
quest.failed: Try more episodes or tune the learning rate!

feel.title: Where You Are vs What You Do
feel.instruction: Navigate the grid. Think: does the direction matter, or does location determine your fate?
feel.question: Think about it: in some spots, it barely matters which way you go (trapped in a dead end). In others, one direction is clearly best. Can you feel the difference?
feel.vHint: How good is just BEING here, regardless of direction?
feel.aHint: How much BETTER is one direction compared to the average?
feel.segue: That feeling — 'this place is bad no matter what I do' — is exactly what the State Value captures!

meet.slide0desc: Standard DQN outputs one Q-value per action. Dueling DQN says: let's split that into two parts — how good is this STATE, and how good is this ACTION here?
meet.slide0example: Dead end: all directions are bad because the STATE is bad. Open path: direction RIGHT has a big advantage over others.
meet.slide1a: V(s) asks: 'How good is it to be in this state at all?' Not 'what should I do' — just 'where am I?'
meet.slide1analogy: It's like standing in a room — some rooms are inherently dangerous (near a cliff), others are safe (near the exit). That's V(s).
meet.slide1b: If the state value is very negative, no action can save you much. If it's very positive, most actions will work out fine.
meet.slide2a: A(s,a) asks: 'How much BETTER is action a compared to the average action in this state?'
meet.slide2analogy: Same room — now which door should you take? That extra nudge above average is the Advantage.
meet.slide2why: The mean is subtracted so advantages stay centered at zero. Otherwise they'd drift together with V(s).
meet.summary1: V(s) captures how good a state is regardless of action — crucial for states where actions barely differ
meet.summary2: A(s,a) captures relative action quality — efficiently identifies the best move
meet.summary3: Combined: Q(s,a) = V(s) + A(s,a) − mean(A) — the same Q-value, learned more efficiently

watch.title: Dueling DQN in Action
watch.desc: Watch V(s) and A(s,a) split in real time as the agent trains!

quest.title: The Analyst's Exam
quest.briefing: Train Dueling DQN and compare it against standard DQN. Dueling DQN should improve performance by better separating state value from action advantage.
quest.rule1: Both DQN and Dueling DQN train on the same grid
quest.rule2: Dueling DQN scores ≥20% better → S rank, ≥10% → A rank
quest.rule3: Ties or slight improvement still earn a B — even matching is a win!
quest.passed: The Analyst's verdict: Dueling DQN separates state value from action advantage for smarter learning!
quest.failed: Try more episodes or a different learning rate!

feel.title: Feel the Problem: Map Your Instincts
feel.instruction: Navigate the grid manually. Notice how certain moves feel 'obviously right' — that instinct is what REINFORCE tries to learn.
feel.controls: Use arrow keys or buttons to move
feel.hint: Try to reach the exit. Avoid traps! Notice which directions feel natural in each spot.
feel.outOfMoves: Out of moves! The agent needs to learn a better route.
feel.segue: Each 'obvious right move' you felt? That's exactly what REINFORCE learns — which actions to repeat!

meet.slide0desc: Imagine learning to throw darts. When you hit the bullseye, your brain says 'do that again!' When you miss badly, it says 'less of that.' REINFORCE works the same way — reward good episodes, penalize bad ones.
meet.slide0key: No table of values. No separate critic. Just: did that work? Yes → do more of it.
meet.slide1title: The Policy is a Probability Distribution
meet.slide1desc: Instead of saying 'always go right', the policy says 'go right 60% of the time, up 30%, down 10%.' These probabilities shift as the agent learns.
meet.slide1note: Click the buttons to see how probabilities shift after good and bad experiences!
meet.slide2desc: After each episode, for every action taken, we update its probability based on how good the total episode return G was:
meet.formulaNote: G is the total discounted reward for the episode. Positive G → increase probability. Negative G → decrease probability.
meet.alphaDesc: Learning rate — how big each step is
meet.returnDesc: Total episode return — was this episode good?
meet.policyDesc: Policy log-prob — we nudge this up or down
meet.slide3title: Why Wait for the Whole Episode?
meet.slide3desc: Unlike Q-Learning which updates every step, REINFORCE waits until the episode ends to compute G. That's the Monte Carlo approach:
meet.step1: Play a full episode from start to finish
meet.step2: Calculate the total return G from each timestep backward
meet.step3: Update all action probabilities proportional to G
meet.slide3note: This is 'Monte Carlo' because we use the actual sampled episode, not a prediction. More accurate but higher variance.
meet.summary1: Direct policy optimization — no value table needed. The policy IS the output.
meet.summary2: Reward-weighted updates: repeat what worked, avoid what didn't. Simple but effective.
meet.summary3: High variance is the tradeoff — results jump around because whole episodes differ. The fix? Add a critic!

quest.title: The Dart Tournament
quest.briefing: Train a REINFORCE agent to navigate the grid world. Find the right learning rate — too small and it learns slowly, too large and it overshoots and forgets!
quest.rule1: Train REINFORCE for 200 episodes on the grid world
quest.rule2: Tune the learning rate to find stable improvement
quest.rule3: Final rank based on average reward of the last 30 episodes
quest.tip: Hint: A learning rate around 0.01–0.03 usually works well. Too high (> 0.05) causes chaos!
quest.passed: Your dart-thrower found its groove! REINFORCE converged to a solid policy.
quest.failed: Try a different learning rate and train again!

feel.title: Feel the Problem: Rate Your Own Moves
feel.instruction: Navigate the grid. After each move, a 'critic' will rate how good that position is. Compare your gut feeling vs the critic's score!
feel.hint: Pay attention to the ratings. Does being near the exit feel different from being near a trap?
feel.escaped: You escaped in {{moves}} moves! The critic's scores guided you well.
feel.outOfMoves: Out of moves! Notice how the critic rated your final position.
feel.segue: That rating system is exactly what the Critic network learns — estimating how good each state is!

meet.slide0desc: A martial artist (Actor) learns fighting moves. A wise master (Critic) watches and gives feedback after each technique: 'That was better than expected!' or 'That was weaker than I expected.' Both get better with every exchange.
meet.actorDesc: Chooses actions. Wants to maximize reward. Learns which moves to make more or less likely.
meet.criticDesc: Evaluates states. Estimates V(s) — how good is it to be HERE? Gives feedback to the Actor.
meet.slide1note: They train simultaneously. The Critic's accuracy helps the Actor learn faster.
meet.slide2desc: The key insight: instead of waiting for a full episode return (like REINFORCE), use a one-step TD error:
meet.tdNote: If δ > 0, the outcome was BETTER than expected — increase the action's probability. If δ < 0, it was worse — decrease it.
meet.slide2key: The Critic's TD error replaces the noisy episode return G — reducing variance and enabling online (every-step) learning!
meet.slide3desc: Watch both algorithms on the same grid. Actor-Critic should learn more smoothly thanks to the Critic's guidance.
meet.slide3note: Actor-Critic typically shows less variance in its reward curve — the Critic keeps updates focused.
meet.summary1: Two networks working together: the Actor acts, the Critic advises. Both improve each other.
meet.summary2: TD error as feedback signal — no need to wait for full episode return. Update happens every step.
meet.summary3: Lower variance than REINFORCE because the Critic's baseline removes noise from the reward signal.

quest.title: Master and Apprentice Challenge
quest.briefing: Race Actor-Critic against REINFORCE on the same grid. The Actor-Critic pair should outperform plain REINFORCE by learning faster and more stably.
quest.rule1: Both Actor-Critic and REINFORCE train for 250 episodes
quest.rule2: Rank based on Actor-Critic's advantage over REINFORCE (AC avg - RF avg)
quest.rule3: S: advantage ≥ 0.3 reward, A: ≥ 0.15, B: ≥ 0.0 (ties count!)
quest.tip: Try actor LR ≈ 0.01, critic LR ≈ 0.05. The critic learns faster than the actor.
quest.passed: The master-apprentice bond is forged! Actor-Critic outperformed plain REINFORCE.
quest.failed: Try tuning the learning rates — actor and critic learn at different speeds!

feel.title: Feel the Power of Parallel Workers
feel.instruction: Launch different numbers of workers to solve the maze. See how parallelism speeds up learning!
feel.hint: More workers → more diverse experience → faster learning. But is 8x workers really 8x faster?
feel.segue: Parallelism gives diminishing returns — but even 2x workers shows clear benefit. Now let's see the real A2C algorithm!

meet.slide0desc: Imagine training an army of soldiers, each sent to explore a different part of the battlefield. They all share one brain — every lesson learned by one immediately helps all the others. That's Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C).
meet.point1: Multiple parallel workers collect experience simultaneously — each sees different parts of the environment
meet.point2: All workers share the SAME policy network — learning from all is instantly available to all
meet.point3: Synchronous updates: all workers finish their episode batch, then update together
meet.slide1note: The synchronized update is key — it keeps training stable, unlike the asynchronous A3C.
meet.slide2desc: Instead of raw returns, A2C uses the 'advantage' — how much better was this action than the typical value of this state?
meet.advantageNote: V(s) is the Critic's estimate of state value. The advantage tells us: was this action above or below average?
meet.positiveAdv: Better than expected — do more of this!
meet.negativeAdv: Worse than expected — do less of this!
meet.slide3title: A2C vs A3C: Sync vs Async
meet.slide3desc: Both use parallel workers, but they differ in HOW they update:
meet.a2cSync: Synchronous: all workers finish, then update together. Stable and predictable.
meet.a3cAsync: Asynchronous: workers update whenever they finish. Faster but unstable on modern hardware.
meet.slide3note: A2C is now preferred over A3C because modern CPUs handle sync parallelism efficiently.
meet.summary1: Parallel workers explore different parts of the environment simultaneously — more diverse experience per update
meet.summary2: Shared policy network: every worker's experience benefits everyone immediately
meet.summary3: Advantage function reduces variance further — only 'above average' actions get reinforced

quest.title: Army of Clones Challenge
quest.briefing: Deploy multiple workers to train faster! Each worker explores independently but shares learning with all others.
quest.rule1: Each worker runs 150 episodes on the grid world
quest.rule2: Rank based on average reward across all workers (last 20 eps each)
quest.rule3: S: avg ≥ -3, A: ≥ -5, B: ≥ -8 — more workers = easier to achieve S!
quest.tip: Start with 8 workers and LR ≈ 0.01 for the best results. Parallel experience is powerful!
quest.passed: The army marches! Your parallel workers conquered the grid world!
quest.failed: Try more workers or a different learning rate!

feel.title: Feel the Problem: Goldilocks Learning
feel.instruction: Try different step sizes. Find the Goldilocks zone — not too small, not too large!
feel.selectStep: Select a step size (learning rate) and see the effect:
feel.resultSlow: Progress is glacial. Learning, but incredibly slowly...
feel.resultGood: Smooth and steady! The agent makes consistent progress.
feel.resultChaos: CRASH! The update was too large — the agent forgot everything it learned!
feel.insight: PPO solves this by clipping the update size. No matter what the gradient says, the policy can't change more than ε in any direction.

meet.slide0desc: You're learning to skateboard. Your coach says: 'Lean forward more.' But if you lean ALL the way forward immediately, you crash. Good coaching means: improve — but not too much at once.
meet.slide0insight: PPO is that careful coach. It prevents policy updates from being too aggressive.
meet.slide1title: The Problem with Vanilla Policy Gradient
meet.slide1desc: Standard policy gradients can take huge steps that destroy what was already learned:
meet.smallStepDesc: Learns very slowly. The policy barely changes each update.
meet.bigStepDesc: Catastrophic forgetting! One bad update can ruin a well-trained policy.
meet.slide1note: The ideal is in between — but how do we enforce that? That's where clipping comes in.
meet.slide2desc: PPO computes a ratio r = π_new(a|s) / π_old(a|s), then CLIPS it within [1-ε, 1+ε]:
meet.clipNote: The min() takes the pessimistic view: we only allow an update if it's beneficial AND not too large.
meet.slide3desc: Same environment, same goal — but very different training curves:
meet.reinforceDesc: Wild swings up and down. One bad update can send it backwards.
meet.ppoDesc: Smoother ascent. Constraints prevent catastrophic drops.
meet.slide3note: PPO is now the default algorithm at OpenAI, DeepMind, and most RL practitioners.
meet.summary1: Clipped surrogate objective prevents too-large updates — the 'learning speed limit'
meet.summary2: Probability ratio r = π_new / π_old measures how much the policy changed — clip if too far from 1
meet.summary3: Multi-epoch reuse: PPO replays collected experience multiple times per update — more data efficiency

quest.title: The Stability Trial
quest.briefing: Train PPO on the quest grid. Find the right clip epsilon — too tight means slow learning, too loose means instability!
quest.rule1: Train PPO for 300 episodes on the grid world
quest.rule2: Tune clip epsilon ε and actor learning rate for stable improvement
quest.rule3: S rank: avg reward ≥ -3 AND stability (std dev ≤ 4). Higher = better!
quest.hintTooSmall: Very small ε: PPO will be very conservative — might not learn much.
quest.hintGood: Good range! This allows meaningful updates while preventing catastrophic changes.
quest.hintTooLarge: Large ε: Updates may be too aggressive, losing PPO's stability benefits.
quest.tip: Recommended: ε = 0.2, LR = 0.01. These are the 'default' PPO hyperparameters for a reason!
quest.passed: Steady hand! Your PPO agent trained stably and reached a strong policy.
quest.failed: Try adjusting ε closer to 0.2 and training again!

boss.title: The Chaos Volcano
boss.intro: The volcano erupts in phases, each more dangerous than the last. You must choose the RIGHT algorithm for each phase — or fail! Each phase rewards a different policy strategy.
boss.phase1desc: Phase 1 — Simple terrain: A familiar grid world. REINFORCE, Actor-Critic, or PPO?
boss.phase2desc: Phase 2 — Harder terrain: A more complex maze. The algorithm that's just 'good enough' may not survive.
boss.phase3desc: Phase 3 — Maximum intensity: Push your best policy to the limit!
boss.passed: The Chaos Volcano is conquered! Your algorithm choices proved masterful.
boss.failed: The volcano wins this time. Study each phase and choose your algorithms more carefully!

selector.reinforceDesc: Simple, high variance. Good baseline but may struggle on harder phases.
selector.actorcriticDesc: Critic reduces variance. Better stability than REINFORCE alone.
selector.ppoDesc: Clipped updates, most stable. Best for phases where consistency matters.

boss.name: The Chaos Volcano
boss.desc: A volcanic eruption where the environment changes every N steps.

reinforce.name: Port Reinforce
reinforce.metaphor: Learning to throw darts — if it hits, do more of that!

actorcritic.name: Port Critic
actorcritic.metaphor: A martial artist (Actor) with a wise master (Critic) giving real-time feedback

a2c.name: Port Parallel
a2c.metaphor: Training an army of robots that share notes to learn faster

ppo.name: Port Proximal
ppo.metaphor: Don't change too much at once — careful steps so you never forget what you learned

continuous.theme: The World Isn't Black and White

boss.name: The Precision Gauntlet
boss.desc: A multi-stage obstacle course requiring the right algorithm for each phase.
